engineering.translation: This verse names a structural impossibility. The resolved one-pointed intelligence of 2.41 — the precondition for buddhi-yoga — cannot arise in an operator whose discriminating mind has already been captured by the flowery promo-cycle of the prior two verses. Notice carefully what is and is not being said. The teaching does not claim status-attached engineers are bad people. It does not coach humility. It names a causal chain. Attachment to enjoyment-of-attainment, plus the lordship-feeling of being the promoted one, captures the mind; that capture blocks the gathered, collected state Shankara reads as 'mind ready for Knowledge' and Ramanuja reads as 'calmed mind'; and without that gathered state, the resolved intelligence simply cannot establish itself. A senior optimising for promotion or for manager-impressing demos fails to focus on the substrate — not from insufficient discipline, but because attention has already been allocated elsewhere. The remedy is not effort. The remedy is dropping the clinging that silently consumes the attention budget.
engineering.concrete_scenario: An engineer sits before a debugger at 3 PM, looking at a memory leak in a Rust service that has grown slowly across three deploys. The bug is genuinely interesting. The pprof profile shows an unfamiliar allocation pattern. The right next move is to read Tokio's spawn-blocking source. The eyes are on screen, but the awareness is not. Half is allocated upward — toward a Slack thread with the manager about a half-promised quarter-end promotion review, contingent on 'visible impact.' A quarter is allocated to framing: whether the eventual fix will read, in the GitHub pull request description, as a director-legible win or an obscure performance internal. The remaining quarter sits inside the debugger. What the work needs is the settled, one-pointed mode — resolved at substrate level: where exactly inside Tokio is the leak actually happening? What is present instead is fragmented: some attention on the bug, some on the promo question, some on framing. The teaching does not advise 'try harder.' It names the causal chain. Capture has consumed the mind; the gathered state cannot form; resolved intelligence cannot establish. The fix is at the root — release the clinging to the promo-frame, do not suppress it. Suppression spends more bandwidth; release returns it.
engineering.falsifiability: The analog fails if a reader concludes that wanting career advancement is itself the pathology, or that the cure is a kind of monkish detachment from organisational outcomes. It is not. Krishna does not condemn svarga; he names attachment-capture as a focus-blocker. An engineer who notices the promo question, sets it aside until the leak is found, and then writes the PR description carefully has done the dropping the verse calls for. An engineer who claims to be unconcerned with promos but cannot actually focus has not dropped anything; they have suppressed and continue to leak attention. The test is whether attention is fully present at the substrate, not whether the operator can recite a renunciation.
engineering.counter_example: When the operator's attention is genuinely captured by something load-bearing — an ongoing incident, a family emergency, a decision the team is waiting on — split attention is correct, not pathological. The verse does not bless single-tasking as a virtue; it names a specific failure mode where the splitter is attachment-to-status-fruits. If the splitting cause is real engineering or real life, this teaching applies differently — it would point at the operator's relationship to the splitter, not at the act of splitting.
engineering.implication: When focus is failing on a substrate-level problem, do not start by accusing yourself of insufficient discipline. First ask what attachment is currently consuming your attention budget. If the answer is the next promotion, the next demo, the next manager-impressing artifact — naming it precisely is the start of the dropping. The gate into buddhi-yoga is opened by attention-release, not by effort.
engineering.quotable_line: Status-attachment is not a moral failure; it is an attention-budget allocation that prevents the resolved buddhi from establishing — the fix is dropping, not suppression.

iterations.mutation: v0 generation: BG 2.44 names the consequence of 2.42-43's veda-vāda-rata pathology — the resolved buddhi cannot establish because attention has been captured. CRITICAL CARE: per task spec, this verse must NOT sound like 'be humble' coaching content. It is specifically about status-attachment as a focus-blocker — a structural-causal claim, not a virtue claim. Engineering layer therefore frames it as an attention-budget allocation problem: bhoga-aiśvarya-prasakti consumes the cetas, samādhi-state cannot form, resolved buddhi cannot establish. Concrete scenario uses a debugger session split between a Rust memory leak (substrate-level work), a Slack promo thread (status-attachment), and PR-framing concerns (aiśvarya-attachment). Named tools: Rust, pprof, Tokio, Slack, GitHub. Falsifiability handles 'wanting advancement is the pathology' (no — attachment-capture is). Counter-example carves out legitimately split attention (real incident, family emergency). Quotable_line names the specific structural-not-moral framing. Tags: focus-practice, deep-work, non-attachment-to-praise.

v2 editorial pass: replaced technical Sanskrit terms in engineering register with English equivalents (vyavasāyātmikā buddhi → resolved one-pointed intelligence; cetas → discriminating mind; samādhi → gathered/collected state, with Shankara/Ramanuja glosses kept as in-line reading; bhoga-aiśvarya-prasakti → 'attachment to enjoyment-of-attainment plus lordship-feeling'). Split compound noun-stacking into discrete sentences. Causal chain phrasing preserved verbatim — the structural-not-moral framing is the verse's load-bearing claim and is unchanged.
